##### Katy Manning | July 29, 2022

# Chemical Vapor Deposition and its Application

##### Katy Manning | July 29, 2022

# Chemical Vapor Deposition and its Application

![Chemical Vapor Deposition and its Application](https://img.imageboss.me/provac/width/700/dpr:2/shop/articles/cvd_700x.jpg?v=1659392680)

### CVD Process

A reactant vapor and dilute inert gases are directed into the reaction chamber at a specified flow rate at a high temperature through diffusion. The reactants and gas molecules now split into films and precursors which then diffuse and adhere to the growth surface of the substrate. Due to the chemical reactions of the gas molecules on the surface, they form a film on the surface. During the CVD process, diffusion takes place where the gaseous reactants go through the boundary layer (atmospheric gas) and adsorb on the substrate. This leads to a chemical reaction, producing the coating film that is deposited on the substrate. The coating film is fairly adherent due to the chemical reactions, since the substrate acts as a catalyst for the chemical reaction to take place.   
  
It is important to note that the reactions in CVD occur in the gas phase to produce a solid coating on the wafer and the product has poor uniformity with several particles. Make sure that the reaction occurs on the wafer so that the coating is deposited on the wafer surface.

There are different types of CVD depending on the operating pressure and heating system. If a chamber uses a heating system that heats up the walls of the chamber and the substrate simultaneously, it is referred to as a hot wall. This is because there are heaters on both sides of the reactor walls. Films are usually deposited on both the substrate and the wall chambers. If the heating system is only at the substrate level and not walls, that is referred to as cold-wall.   
  
As for operating pressure CVDs, we have the atmospheric pressure CVDs that operate under atmospheric pressure, low pressure CVDs operate at a higher temperature than atmospheric pressure CVD, while the Plasma Enhanced CVD operates under low pressure, but does not depend on thermal energy to accelerate the reaction process.

### How is CVD used to coat glass?

CVD can be applied to glass, ceramics, and metals. For glass coating, Pyrolytic online coating is utilized. This is a CVD process made in the bath section of the float line in such a way that once the glass enters the tin bath, its temperature is usually high, about 1049° Celsius and as it goes to the exit end of the bath, the temperature reduces to about 605° Celsius and a coating is formed on the glass. Silane gas together with nitrogen is introduced close to the glass surface, and due to the temperature, a chemical reaction takes place where pure silicon condenses on the glass to form a hard, durable coating.

### Applications of CVD processes

CVD processes are used by many industries such as the aircraft and automotive industries. They are used to modify surfaces to promote adhesion. Through the CVD process, coatings increase the longevity of materials, such as making metals resistant to rust and corrosion. CVD is used in the semiconductor industry to make materials that are used to produce solar panels, light-emitting diodes, and integrated circuits found in devices like phones and televisions. CVD also helps prepare single crystal metal oxides, such as sapphire and ferrites. Lastly, it is through the CVD process that net shape products such as tubes and crucibles are produced. The substrate is removed and leaves a free-standing thin material.

--- SOURCE 2: Chemical vapor deposition - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_vapor_deposition

SUMMARY:
Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is a vacuum deposition method used to produce high-quality, and high-performance, solid materials. The process is often used

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Chemical vapor deposition

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/PICT0111.JPG/250px-PICT0111.JPG)

**Chemical vapor deposition** (**CVD**) is a [vacuum deposition](/wiki/Vacuum_deposition "Vacuum deposition") method used to produce high-quality, and high-performance, solid materials. The process is often used in the [semiconductor industry](/wiki/Semiconductor_industry "Semiconductor industry") to produce [thin films](/wiki/Thin_film "Thin film").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In typical CVD, the [wafer](/wiki/Wafer_(electronics) "Wafer (electronics)") (substrate) is exposed to one or more [volatile](/wiki/Volatility_(chemistry) "Volatility (chemistry)") [precursors](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/precursor "wikt:precursor"), which [react](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") and/or [decompose](/wiki/Chemical_decomposition "Chemical decomposition") on the substrate surface to produce the desired deposit. Frequently, volatile [by-products](/wiki/By-product "By-product") are also produced, which are removed by gas flow through the reaction chamber.

[Microfabrication](/wiki/Microfabrication "Microfabrication") processes widely use CVD to deposit materials in various forms, including: [monocrystalline](/wiki/Single_crystal "Single crystal"), [polycrystalline](/wiki/Polycrystalline "Polycrystalline"), [amorphous](/wiki/Amorphous "Amorphous"), and [epitaxial](/wiki/Epitaxy "Epitaxy"). These materials include: [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") ([dioxide](/wiki/Silicon_dioxide "Silicon dioxide"), [carbide](/wiki/Silicon_carbide "Silicon carbide"), [nitride](/wiki/Silicon_nitride "Silicon nitride"), [oxynitride](/wiki/Silicon_oxynitride "Silicon oxynitride")), carbon ([fiber](/wiki/Carbon_(fiber) "Carbon (fiber)"), [nanofibers](/wiki/Carbon_nanofibers "Carbon nanofibers"), [nanotubes](/wiki/Carbon_nanotube "Carbon nanotube"), [diamond](/wiki/Synthetic_diamond "Synthetic diamond") and [graphene](/wiki/Graphene "Graphene")), [fluorocarbons](/wiki/Fluorocarbon "Fluorocarbon"), [filaments](/wiki/Electrical_filament "Electrical filament"), [tungsten](/wiki/Tungsten "Tungsten"), [titanium nitride](/wiki/Titanium_nitride "Titanium nitride") and various [high-κ dielectrics](/wiki/High-%CE%BA_dielectric "High-κ dielectric").

The term *chemical vapour deposition* was coined in 1960 by *John M. Blocher, Jr.* who intended to differentiate *chemical* from *[physical vapour deposition](/wiki/Physical_vapour_deposition "Physical vapour deposition")* (PVD).

## Types

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/ThermalCVD-en.svg/250px-ThermalCVD-en.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/PlasmaCVD-en.svg/250px-PlasmaCVD-en.svg.png)

CVD is practiced in a variety of formats. These processes generally differ in the means by which chemical reactions are initiated.

Most modern CVD is either LPCVD or UHVCVD.

## Uses

CVD is commonly used to deposit conformal films and augment substrate surfaces in ways that more traditional surface modification techniques are not capable of. CVD is extremely useful in the process of [atomic layer deposition](/wiki/Atomic_layer_deposition "Atomic layer deposition") at depositing extremely thin layers of material. A variety of applications for such films exist. [Gallium arsenide](/wiki/Gallium_arsenide "Gallium arsenide") is used in some [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") (ICs) and photovoltaic devices. Amorphous polysilicon is used in photovoltaic devices. Certain [carbides](/wiki/Carbide "Carbide") and [nitrides](/wiki/Nitride "Nitride") confer wear-resistance.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Polymerization by CVD, perhaps the most versatile of all applications, allows for super-thin coatings which possess some very desirable qualities, such as lubricity, hydrophobicity and weather-resistance to name a few.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The CVD of [metal-organic frameworks](/wiki/Metal-organic_framework "Metal-organic framework"), a class of crystalline nanoporous materials, has recently been demonstrated.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Recently scaled up as an integrated cleanroom process depositing large-area substrates,[[13]](#cite_note-13) the applications for these films are anticipated in gas sensing and [low-κ dielectrics](/wiki/Low-%CE%BA_dielectric "Low-κ dielectric"). CVD techniques are advantageous for membrane coatings as well, such as those in desalination or water treatment, as these coatings can be sufficiently uniform (conformal) and thin that they do not clog membrane pores.[[14]](#cite_note-Servi_Guillen-Burrieza_Warsinger_Livernois_2017_pp._470–479-14)

## Commercially important materials prepared by CVD

### Polysilicon

[Polycrystalline silicon](/wiki/Polycrystalline_silicon "Polycrystalline silicon") is deposited from [trichlorosilane](/wiki/Trichlorosilane "Trichlorosilane") (SiHCl3) or [silane](/wiki/Silane "Silane") (SiH4), using the following reactions:[[15]](#cite_note-Ullmann-15)

This reaction is usually performed in LPCVD systems, with either pure silane feedstock, or a solution of silane with 70–80% [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen"). Temperatures between 600 and 650 °C and pressures between 25 and 150 Pa yield a growth rate between 10 and 20 [nm](/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre") per minute. An alternative process uses a [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen")-based solution. The hydrogen reduces the growth rate, but the temperature is raised to 850 or even 1050 °C to compensate. Polysilicon may be grown directly with doping, if gases such as [phosphine](/wiki/Phosphine "Phosphine"), [arsine](/wiki/Arsine "Arsine") or [diborane](/wiki/Diborane "Diborane") are added to the CVD chamber. Diborane increases the growth rate, but arsine and phosphine decrease it.

### Silicon dioxide

Silicon dioxide (usually called simply "oxide" in the semiconductor industry) may be deposited by several different processes. Common source gases include [silane](/wiki/Silane "Silane") and [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"), [dichlorosilane](/wiki/Dichlorosilane "Dichlorosilane") (SiCl2H2) and [nitrous oxide](/wiki/Nitrous_oxide "Nitrous oxide")[[16]](#cite_note-16) (N2O), or [tetraethylorthosilicate](/wiki/Tetraethylorthosilicate "Tetraethylorthosilicate") (TEOS; Si(OC2H5)4). The reactions are as follows:[[17]](#cite_note-cao:2011-17)

The choice of source gas depends on the thermal stability of the substrate; for instance, [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium") is sensitive to high temperature. Silane deposits between 300 and 500 °C, dichlorosilane at around 900 °C, and TEOS between 650 and 750 °C, resulting in a layer of *low- temperature oxide* (LTO). However, silane produces a lower-quality oxide than the other methods (lower [dielectric strength](/wiki/Dielectric_strength "Dielectric strength"), for instance), and it deposits non[conformally](/wiki/Conformal_film "Conformal film"). Any of these reactions may be used in LPCVD, but the silane reaction is also done in APCVD. CVD oxide invariably has lower quality than [thermal oxide](/wiki/Thermal_oxidation "Thermal oxidation"), but thermal oxidation can only be used in the earliest stages of IC manufacturing.

Oxide may also be grown with impurities ([alloying](/wiki/Alloy "Alloy") or "[doping](/wiki/Doping_(semiconductor) "Doping (semiconductor)")"). This may have two purposes. During further process steps that occur at high temperature, the impurities may [diffuse](/wiki/Atomic_diffusion "Atomic diffusion") from the oxide into adjacent layers (most notably silicon) and dope them. Oxides containing 5–15% impurities by mass are often used for this purpose. In addition, silicon dioxide alloyed with [phosphorus pentoxide](/wiki/Phosphorus_pentoxide "Phosphorus pentoxide") ("P-glass") can be used to smooth out uneven surfaces. P-glass softens and reflows at temperatures above 1000 °C. This process requires a phosphorus concentration of at least 6%, but concentrations above 8% can corrode aluminium. Phosphorus is deposited from phosphine gas and oxygen:

[Glasses](/wiki/Glass "Glass") containing both boron and phosphorus (borophosphosilicate glass, BPSG) undergo viscous flow at lower temperatures; around 850 °C is achievable with glasses containing around 5 weight % of both constituents, but stability in air can be difficult to achieve. Phosphorus oxide in high concentrations interacts with ambient moisture to produce phosphoric acid. Crystals of BPO4 can also precipitate from the flowing glass on cooling; these crystals are not readily etched in the standard reactive plasmas used to pattern oxides, and will result in circuit defects in integrated circuit manufacturing.

Besides these intentional impurities, CVD oxide may contain byproducts of the deposition. TEOS produces a relatively pure oxide, whereas silane introduces hydrogen impurities, and dichlorosilane introduces [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine").

Lower temperature deposition of silicon dioxide and doped glasses from TEOS using ozone rather than oxygen has also been explored (350 to 500 °C). Ozone glasses have excellent conformality but tend to be hygroscopic – that is, they absorb water from the air due to the incorporation of silanol (Si-OH) in the glass. Infrared spectroscopy and mechanical strain as a function of temperature are valuable diagnostic tools for diagnosing such problems.

#### Silicon nitride

Silicon nitride is often used as an insulator and chemical barrier in manufacturing ICs. The following two reactions deposit silicon nitride from the gas phase:

Silicon nitride deposited by LPCVD contains up to 8% hydrogen. It also experiences strong tensile [stress](/wiki/Stress_(physics) "Stress (physics)"), which may crack films thicker than 200 nm. However, it has higher [resistivity](/wiki/Resistivity "Resistivity") and dielectric strength than most insulators commonly available in microfabrication (1016 [Ω](/wiki/Ohm "Ohm")·cm and 10 M[V](/wiki/Volt "Volt")/cm, respectively).

Another two reactions may be used in plasma to deposit SiNH:

These films have much less tensile stress, but worse electrical properties (resistivity 106 to 1015 Ω·cm, and dielectric strength 1 to 5 MV/cm).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Metals

Tungsten CVD, used for forming conductive contacts, vias, and plugs on a semiconductor device,[[19]](#cite_note-19) is achieved from [tungsten hexafluoride](/wiki/Tungsten_hexafluoride "Tungsten hexafluoride") (WF6), which may be deposited in two ways:

Other metals, notably aluminium and [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), can be deposited by CVD. As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chemical_vapor_deposition&action=edit), commercially cost-effective CVD for copper did not exist, although volatile sources exist, such as Cu([hfac](/wiki/Hexafluoroacetylacetone "Hexafluoroacetylacetone"))2. Copper is typically deposited by [electroplating](/wiki/Electroplating "Electroplating"). Aluminium can be deposited from [triisobutylaluminium](/wiki/Triisobutylaluminium "Triisobutylaluminium") (TIBAL) and related [organoaluminium compounds](/wiki/Organoaluminium_compound "Organoaluminium compound").

CVD for [molybdenum](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum"), [tantalum](/wiki/Tantalum "Tantalum"), [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium"), nickel is widely used.[[20]](#cite_note-20) These metals can form useful [silicides](/wiki/Silicide "Silicide") when deposited onto silicon. Mo, Ta and Ti are deposited by LPCVD, from their pentachlorides. Nickel, molybdenum, and tungsten can be deposited at low temperatures from their carbonyl precursors. In general, for an arbitrary metal *M*, the chloride deposition reaction is as follows:

whereas the carbonyl decomposition reaction can happen spontaneously under thermal treatment or acoustic cavitation and is as follows:

the decomposition of metal carbonyls is often violently precipitated by moisture or air, where oxygen reacts with the metal precursor to form metal or metal oxide along with carbon dioxide.

[Niobium(V) oxide](/wiki/Niobium(V)_oxide "Niobium(V) oxide") layers can be produced by the thermal decomposition of [niobium(V) ethoxide](/wiki/Niobium(V)_ethoxide "Niobium(V) ethoxide") with the loss of [diethyl ether](/wiki/Diethyl_ether "Diethyl ether")[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) according to the equation:

### Graphene

Many variations of CVD can be utilized to synthesize graphene. Although many advancements have been made, the processes listed below are not commercially viable yet.

The most popular carbon source that is used to produce graphene is methane gas. One of the less popular choices is petroleum asphalt, notable for being inexpensive but more difficult to work with.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

Although methane is the most popular carbon source, hydrogen is required during the preparation process to promote carbon deposition on the substrate. If the flow ratio of methane and hydrogen are not appropriate, it will cause undesirable results. During the growth of graphene, the role of methane is to provide a carbon source, the role of hydrogen is to provide H atoms to corrode amorphous C,[[24]](#cite_note-24) and improve the quality of graphene. But excessive H atoms can also corrode graphene.[[25]](#cite_note-25) As a result, the integrity of the crystal lattice is destroyed, and the quality of graphene is deteriorated.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Therefore, by optimizing the flow rate of methane and hydrogen gases in the growth process, the quality of graphene can be improved.

The use of catalyst is viable in changing the physical process of graphene production. Notable examples include iron nanoparticles, nickel foam, and gallium vapor. These catalysts can either be used in situ during graphene buildup,[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27) or situated at some distance away at the deposition area.[[28]](#cite_note-:2-28) Some catalysts require another step to remove them from the sample material.[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27)

The direct growth of high-quality, large single-crystalline domains of graphene on a dielectric substrate is of vital importance for applications in electronics and optoelectronics. Combining the advantages of both catalytic CVD and the ultra-flat dielectric substrate, gaseous catalyst-assisted CVD[[29]](#cite_note-29) paves the way for synthesizing high-quality graphene for device applications while avoiding the transfer process.

Physical conditions such as surrounding pressure, temperature, carrier gas, and chamber material play a big role in production of graphene.

Most systems use LPCVD with pressures ranging from 1 to 1500 Pa.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[28]](#cite_note-:2-28) However, some still use APCVD.[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27) Low pressures are used more commonly as they help prevent unwanted reactions and produce more uniform thickness of deposition on the substrate.

On the other hand, temperatures used range from 800 to 1050 °C.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27) High temperatures translate to an increase of the rate of reaction. Caution has to be exercised as high temperatures do pose higher danger levels in addition to greater energy costs.

Hydrogen gas and inert gases such as argon are flowed into the system.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[27]](#cite_note-:1-27) These gases act as a carrier, enhancing surface reaction and improving reaction rate, thereby increasing deposition of graphene onto the substrate.

Standard quartz tubing and chambers are used in CVD of graphene.[[30]](#cite_note-:3-30)[[31]](#cite_note-:4-31) Quartz is chosen because it has a very high melting point and is chemically inert. In other words, quartz does not interfere with any physical or chemical reactions regardless of the conditions.

Raman spectroscopy, X-ray spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) are used to examine and characterize the graphene samples.[[30]](#cite_note-:3-30)[[31]](#cite_note-:4-31)

Raman spectroscopy is used to characterize and identify the graphene particles; X-ray spectroscopy is used to characterize chemical states; TEM is used to provide fine details regarding the internal composition of graphene; SEM is used to examine the surface and topography.

Sometimes, atomic force microscopy (AFM) is used to measure local properties such as friction and magnetism.[[30]](#cite_note-:3-30)[[31]](#cite_note-:4-31)

Cold wall CVD technique can be used to study the underlying surface science involved in graphene nucleation and growth as it allows unprecedented control of process parameters like gas flow rates, temperature and pressure as demonstrated in a recent study. The study was carried out in a home-built vertical cold wall system utilizing resistive heating by passing direct current through the substrate. It provided conclusive insight into a typical surface-mediated nucleation and growth mechanism involved in two-dimensional materials grown using catalytic CVD under conditions sought out in the semiconductor industry.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Graphene nanoribbon

In spite of graphene's exciting electronic and thermal properties, it is unsuitable as a transistor for future digital devices, due to the absence of a bandgap between the conduction and valence bands. This makes it impossible to switch between on and off states with respect to electron flow. Scaling things down, graphene nanoribbons of less than 10 nm in width do exhibit electronic bandgaps and are therefore potential candidates for digital devices. Precise control over their dimensions, and hence electronic properties, however, represents a challenging goal, and the ribbons typically possess rough edges that are detrimental to their performance.

### Diamond

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Single-crystal_CVD_diamond_disc.jpg/250px-Single-crystal_CVD_diamond_disc.jpg)
![A colorless faceted gem](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Apollo_synthetic_diamond.jpg/250px-Apollo_synthetic_diamond.jpg)

CVD can be used to produce a [synthetic diamond](/wiki/Synthetic_diamond "Synthetic diamond") by creating the circumstances necessary for carbon atoms in a gas to settle on a substrate in crystalline form. CVD of diamonds has received much attention in the materials sciences because it allows many new applications that had previously been considered too expensive. CVD diamond growth typically occurs under low pressure (1–27 [kPa](/wiki/Pascal_(unit) "Pascal (unit)"); 0.145–3.926 [psi](/wiki/Pounds_per_square_inch "Pounds per square inch"); 7.5–203 [Torr](/wiki/Torr "Torr")) and involves feeding varying amounts of gases into a chamber, energizing them and providing conditions for diamond growth on the substrate. The gases always include a carbon source, and typically include hydrogen as well, though the amounts used vary greatly depending on the type of diamond being grown. Energy sources include [hot filament](/wiki/Hot_filament "Hot filament"), [microwave](/wiki/Microwave "Microwave") power, and [arc discharges](/wiki/Electric_arc "Electric arc"), among others. The energy source is intended to generate a plasma in which the gases are broken down and more complex chemistries occur. The actual chemical process for diamond growth is still under study and is complicated by the very wide variety of diamond growth processes used.

Using CVD, films of diamond can be grown over large areas of substrate with control over the properties of the diamond produced. In the past, when high pressure high temperature (HPHT) techniques were used to produce a diamond, the result was typically very small free-standing diamonds of varying sizes. With CVD diamond, growth areas of greater than fifteen centimeters (six inches) in diameter have been achieved, and much larger areas are likely to be successfully coated with diamond in the future. Improving this process is key to enabling several important applications.

The growth of diamond directly on a substrate allows the addition of many of diamond's important qualities to other materials. Since diamond has the highest [thermal conductivity](/wiki/Thermal_conductivity "Thermal conductivity") of any bulk material, layering diamond onto high heat-producing electronics (such as optics and transistors) allows the diamond to be used as a heat sink.[[34]](#cite_note-poly1-34)[[35]](#cite_note-heat2-35) Diamond films are being grown on valve rings, cutting tools, and other objects that benefit from diamond's hardness and exceedingly low wear rate. In each case the diamond growth must be carefully done to achieve the necessary adhesion onto the substrate. Diamond's very high scratch resistance and thermal conductivity, combined with a lower [coefficient of thermal expansion](/wiki/Coefficient_of_thermal_expansion "Coefficient of thermal expansion") than [Pyrex](/wiki/Pyrex "Pyrex") glass, a [coefficient of friction](/wiki/Coefficient_of_friction "Coefficient of friction") close to that of Teflon ([polytetrafluoroethylene](/wiki/Polytetrafluoroethylene "Polytetrafluoroethylene")) and strong [lipophilicity](/wiki/Lipophilicity "Lipophilicity") would make it a nearly ideal non-stick coating for cookware if large substrate areas could be coated economically.

CVD growth allows one to control the properties of the diamond produced. In the area of diamond growth, the word "diamond" is used as a description of any material primarily made up of [sp3-bonded](/wiki/Orbital_hybridisation#sp3 "Orbital hybridisation") carbon, and there are many different types of diamond included in this. By regulating the processing parameters—especially the gases introduced, but also including the pressure the system is operated under, the temperature of the diamond, and the method of generating plasma—many different materials that can be considered diamond can be made. Single-crystal diamond can be made containing various [dopants](/wiki/Dopant "Dopant").[[36]](#cite_note-single-36) [Polycrystalline diamond](/wiki/Synthetic_diamond#Crystallinity "Synthetic diamond") consisting of grain sizes from several [nanometers](/wiki/Nanometer "Nanometer") to several [micrometers](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre") can be grown.[[34]](#cite_note-poly1-34)[[37]](#cite_note-poly2-37) Some polycrystalline diamond grains are surrounded by thin, non-diamond carbon, while others are not. These different factors affect the diamond's hardness, smoothness, conductivity, optical properties and more.

## Chalcogenides

Commercially, [mercury cadmium telluride](/wiki/Mercury_cadmium_telluride "Mercury cadmium telluride") is of continuing interest for detection of [infrared radiation](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared"). Consisting of an [alloy](/wiki/Alloy "Alloy") of CdTe and HgTe, this material can be prepared from the dimethyl derivatives of the respective elements.

SUMMARY:
Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is a cornerstone technology in modern materials engineering, enabling the precise deposition of high-quality thin films and coatings essential for today's advanced manufacturing needs.This sophisticated process involves the controlled reaction of gaseous precursors to form solid materials that adhere to substrate surfaces. At Linde Advanced Material Technologies (AMT), we specialize in providing cutting-edge solutions for a wide range of Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) technologies. Our expertise extends into unique applications like Chemical Vapor Aluminizing (CVA), a high-temperature CVD process that doesn’t just deposit a cold layer of material like in semiconductors but chemically transforms the surface of superalloy turbine components by diffusing aluminum into their structure.

What is Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)?
========================================

Discover how Linde's cutting-edge CVD and CVA technologies enable advanced thin films, coatings, and superalloy surface transformations for aerospace, semiconductors, and more.

![Image 9: Aluminide coating process](https://assets.linde.com/-/media/celum-connect/2025/04/16/13/57/av_tin_aluminide_194339.jpg?impolicy=focal-point&cw=725&ch=363&fx=450&fy=300&r=bc872220ac)

Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is a cornerstone technology in modern materials engineering, enabling the precise deposition of high-quality thin films and coatings essential for today's advanced manufacturing needs.This sophisticated process involves the controlled reaction of gaseous precursors to form solid materials that adhere to substrate surfaces. It delivers exceptional uniformity and performance characteristics that surpass traditional coating methods. CVD technology has evolved to meet increasingly complex specifications across aerospace, automotive, semiconductor, and advanced materials applications.

#### The Fundamentals of CVD

##### Basic Principles and Chemical Reactions

CVD uses controlled chemical reactions in the vapor phase. The process begins by introducing volatile precursor materials into a reaction chamber, which undergoes carefully managed chemical reactions to form the desired solid material on a substrate surface. These reactions can involve thermal decomposition, reduction, oxidation, or compound formation depending on the specific application requirements and desired coating properties. Initiated chemical vapor deposition presents an advanced variation of the process, particularly valuable for creating polymer thin films with precise control over molecular structure and composition.The chemistry behind CVD processes is precisely controlled through parameters such as temperature, pressure, and gas flow rates. This level of control enables the formation of high-purity materials with specific crystalline structures, compositions, and properties. CVD's versatility allows for the deposition of a wide range of materials, including metals, semiconductors, carbides, nitrides, and various compounds, each with unique characteristics suited to specific industrial applications.

##### Key Components and Equipment

CVD systems require sophisticated equipment to maintain precise control over deposition parameters. The essential components include:

*   **Precursor Delivery System -**Integrated equipment for precursor storage and delivery, featuring mass flow controllers and temperature-controlled vessels.
*   **Reaction Chamber -**Engineered deposition environment with temperature control and vacuum systems optimized for uniform gas flow.
*   **Heating System -**Thermal management using resistive, RF induction, or infrared heating for precise temperature control.
*   **Gas Distribution System -** Network of gas lines and controllers managing precursor and carrier gas flow.
*   **Vacuum and Control Systems -**High-performance pumps and instrumentation for maintaining pressure conditions and monitoring process parameters.

##### Step-by-Step Process Overview

The CVD process follows a systematic sequence of steps to achieve optimal deposition results:

1.   **Substrate Preparation -** Surface cleaning and activation to ensure optimal adhesion and film quality.
2.   **Chamber Environment Setup -** Establishing required temperature and pressure conditions with process gases.
3.   **Precursor Introduction -** Controlled delivery of precursor materials with inert carrier gases.
4.   **Gas-Phase Reactions -** Formation of intermediate species through precursor chemical reactions.
5.   **Surface Reactions -** Diffusion and reaction of species at the substrate surface.
6.   **Film Growth -** Controlled material buildup forming uniform coating layers.
7.   **Process Completion -** Removal of byproducts and controlled system cool-down with any required post-processing.

#### Types of Chemical Vapor Deposition Systems

Modern CVD technology encompasses several specialized systems, each optimized for specific applications:

*   **Low-pressure CVD (LPCVD) -** Operates at reduced pressures to enhance film uniformity and reduce unwanted gas-phase reactions. It is particularly valuable for semiconductor manufacturing, where exceptional uniformity is required.
*   **Plasma-Enhanced CVD (PECVD) -** Utilizes plasma activation to enable deposition at lower temperatures, making it ideal for temperature-sensitive substrates and electronics manufacturing.
*   **Metal-Organic CVD (MOCVD) -** Employs metal-organic compounds as precursors, enabling the deposition of compound semiconductors and advanced electronic materials with precise composition control.
*   **Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) -** A specialized form of CVD that achieves atomic-level thickness control through sequential, self-limiting surface reactions.
*   **Hot-Wall CVD -** This type of CVD features heated chamber walls to maintain uniform temperature distribution. It is commonly used for batch processing in semiconductor manufacturing.
*   **Cold-Wall CVD -**Employs selective substrate heating to minimize unwanted deposition on chamber walls, optimizing precursor utilization and reducing maintenance requirements.

#### Applications and Uses of Chemical Vapor Deposition

##### Aerospace Industry

In aerospace applications, CVD technology enables the deposition of critical protective coatings that enhance component performance and longevity. These coatings protect jet engine components against high temperatures, oxidation, and corrosion.

Advanced CVD processes allow for the creation of thermal barrier coatings, wear-resistant surfaces, and specialized materials that meet the rigorous demands of aerospace applications.

##### Automotive Industry

The automotive sector leverages CVD technology to enhance component durability and performance. Applications include the deposition of wear-resistant coatings on engine components, hard carbon coatings for fuel injection systems, and decorative finishes with superior durability.

CVD processes enable the creation of advanced materials that, through reduced friction and enhanced thermal management, contribute to improved fuel efficiency and reduced emissions.

##### Semiconductor Manufacturing and Microelectronics

CVD plays a key role in semiconductor device fabrication, enabling the deposition of dielectric layers, conductive films, and specialized materials essential for modern microelectronics. The process allows for precise film thickness and composition control, which is critical for creating advanced integrated circuits and electronic components. High-purity silicon, silicon dioxide, and various compound semiconductors are routinely deposited using CVD technology.

#### Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology

In advanced materials and nanotechnology, the precise control of CVD processes enables the creation of novel materials with unique properties. Chemical vapor deposition growth techniques have revolutionized the synthesis of two-dimensional materials, from carbon nanotubes to atomically thin graphene layers. The process enables precise layer-by-layer formation of advanced materials such as hexagonal boron nitride, an ideal substrate and insulating material in next-generation electronics.

CVD methods have also become instrumental in producing transition metal dichalcogenides, offering unprecedented control over crystal structure and layer thickness. These advanced materials drive innovation across multiple sectors, from optoelectronics and quantum computing to energy storage and advanced composites.

#### Linde AMT's CVD Technology

At Linde Advanced Material Technologies (AMT), we specialize in providing cutting-edge solutions for a wide range of Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) technologies. Our expertise extends into unique applications like Chemical Vapor Aluminizing (CVA), a high-temperature CVD process that doesn’t just deposit a cold layer of material like in semiconductors but chemically transforms the surface of superalloy turbine components by diffusing aluminum into their structure.

##### Elevating Material Performance with Aluminide Coatings

Aluminide coatings are a type of diffusion surface treatment we offer that is designed to enhance the high-temperature performance of materials. Applied to Nickel- and Cobalt-based turbine airfoils, these coatings improve resistance to oxidation and corrosion, ensuring durable performance in extreme environments. We apply our aluminide coatings to internal and external surfaces through multiple methods, including:

*   Vapor Phase Aluminide (VPA)
*   Platinum Aluminide
*   Pack Cementation
*   Slurry Aluminide

##### Why Partner with Linde?

Linde AMT is steadfastly committed to advancing CVD technology through continuous innovation and development. Our state-of-the-art CVD systems are engineered to deliver exceptional coating quality, process reliability, and operational efficiency. With decades of experience in materials technology and surface engineering, we provide comprehensive CVD solutions that meet the most demanding requirements across various industries.

Our expert team works closely with customers to optimize processes, enhance productivity, and achieve superior coating results. Contact our technical experts today to explore how our CVD technology can advance your manufacturing capabilities and product performance.

#### FAQs

**What are the main advantages of chemical vapor deposition?**

 CVD offers several key advantages, including exceptional coating uniformity, high purity levels, excellent adhesion to substrates, and the ability to coat complex geometries uniformly. The process also provides precise control over coating thickness and composition, enabling customization for specific application requirements.

**How does chemical vapor deposition compare to other coating methods?**

 Chemical vapor deposition features distinct characteristics that set it apart from other coating technologies like physical vapor deposition (PVD). CVD excels at producing uniform coatings on complex geometries and enables strong adhesion to substrates at typically higher deposition rates and temperatures. Each coating method has specific advantages that make it suitable for different applications, allowing manufacturers to select the optimal process based on their requirements.

**What materials can be deposited using chemical vapor deposition?**

 CVD can deposit diverse materials, including pure metals, metal alloys, semiconductors, carbides, nitrides, oxides, and various compounds. Common materials include silicon, tungsten, titanium nitride, silicon dioxide, and diamond-like carbon.

**What factors affect the quality of CVD coatings?**

 Several key parameters influence the quality of CVD coatings, including substrate temperature, chamber pressure, precursor concentration, gas flow rates, and substrate surface condition. Proper control of these factors is essential for achieving desired coating properties such as thickness uniformity, composition, and microstructure.

![The CVD process begins with tanks containing an initiator material (red) and one or more monomers (purple and blue), which are the building blocks of the desired polymer coating. These are vaporized, either by heating them or reducing the pressure, and are then introduced into a vacuum chamber containing the material to be coated. The initiator helps to speed up the process in which the monomers l...](/themes/mit/src/img/placeholder/placeholder--news-article--image-gallery.jpg)

Previous image
Next image

In a sense, says MIT chemical engineering professor Karen Gleason, you can trace the technology of chemical vapor deposition, or CVD, all the way back to prehistory: “When the cavemen lit a lamp and soot was deposited on the wall of a cave,” she says, that was a rudimentary form of CVD.

Today, CVD is a basic tool of manufacturing — used in everything from sunglasses to potato-chip bags — and is fundamental to the production of much of today’s electronics. It is also a technique subject to constant refining and expansion, pushing materials research in new directions — such as the production of large-scale sheets of graphene, or the development of solar cells that could be “printed” onto a sheet of paper or plastic.

In that latter area, Gleason, who also serves as MIT’s associate provost, has been a pioneer. She developed what had traditionally been a high-temperature process used to deposit metals under industrial conditions into a low-temperature process that could be used for more delicate materials, such as organic polymers. That development, a refinement of a method invented in the 1950s by Union Carbide to produce protective polymer coatings, is what enabled, for example, the printable solar cells that Gleason and others have developed.

This vapor deposition of polymers has opened the door to a variety of materials that would be difficult, and in some cases impossible, to produce in any other way. For example, many useful polymers, such as water-shedding materials to protect industrial components or biological implants, are made from precursors that are not soluble, and thus could not be produced using conventional solution-based methods. In addition, says Gleason, the Alexander and I. Michael Kasser Professor at MIT, the CVD process itself induces chemical reactions between coatings and substrates that can strongly bond the material to the surface.

Gleason’s work on polymer-based CVD began in the 1990s, when she did experiments with Teflon, a compound of chlorine and fluorine. That work led to a now-burgeoning field detailed in a new book Gleason edited, titled “CVD Polymers: Fabrication of Organic Surfaces and Devices” (Wiley, 2015).

At the time, the thinking was that the only way to make CVD work with polymer materials was by using plasma — an electrically charged gas — to initiate the reaction. Gleason tried to carry out experiments to prove this, beginning by running a control experiment without the plasma in order to demonstrate how important it was for making the process work. Instead, her control experiment worked just fine with no plasma at all, proving that for many polymers this step was not necessary.

But the equipment Gleason used allowed the temperature of the gas to be controlled separately from that of the substrate; having the substrate cooler turned out to be key. She went on to demonstrate the plasma-free process with more than 70 different polymers, opening up a whole new field of research.

The process can require a lot of fine-tuning, but is fundamentally a simple set of steps: The material to be coated is placed inside a vacuum chamber — which dictates the maximum size of objects that can be coated. Then, the coating material is heated, or the pressure around it is reduced until the material vaporizes, either inside the vacuum chamber or in an adjacent area from which the vapor can be introduced. There, the suspended material begins to settle onto the substrate material and form a uniform coating. Adjusting the temperature and duration of the process makes it possible to control the thickness of the coating.

With metals or metal compounds, such as those used in the semiconductor industry, or the silvery coatings inside snack bags, the heated metal vapor deposits on a cooler substrate. In the polymer process, it’s a bit more complex: Two or more different precursor compounds, called monomers, are introduced into the chamber, where they react to form polymers as they deposit on the surface.

Even high-temperature CVD processing has evolved, with great potential for commercial applications. For example, the research group of John Hart, an associate professor of mechanical engineering, has built a roll-to-roll processing system using CVD to make sheets of graphene, a material with potential applications ranging from large-screen displays to water-filtration systems. Hart’s group and others have used CVD to produce large arrays of carbon nanotubes, materials with potential as new electrodes for batteries or fuel cells.

“It’s a very versatile and widely used manufacturing process,” Hart says, “and a very general process that can be tailored to many different applications.”

One great advantage of CVD processing is that it can create coatings of uniform thickness even over complex shapes. For example, CVD can be used to uniformly coat carbon nanotubes — tiny cylinders of pure carbon that are far more slender than a hair — such as to modify their mechanical properties and make them react chemically to certain substances.

“By combining two CVD processes — one to grow the carbon nanotubes, and another to coat the nanotubes — we have a scalable way to manufacture nanomaterials with new properties,” Hart says.

Much progress in CVD research in recent years traces back to Gleason’s unexpected discovery, back in the 1990s, that the process could work without plasma — and her follow-up on that finding. “You need to pay attention when a new thing happens,” she says. “That’s sort of the key.”

enriched_points.expanded_content: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) is a vacuum deposition method where gaseous precursors react on a solid surface to form high-quality thin films through chemical reactions.
CVD is crucial in electronics for fabricating semiconductors, depositing thin films for integrated circuits, solar cells, and light-emitting diodes, enhancing functionality and durability.
CVD offers superior uniformity, excellent adhesion, and the ability to coat complex geometries, enabling high-purity materials with adjustable thickness and composition tailored to specific needs.
CVD faces challenges such as high equipment costs, the need for precise control of reaction conditions, and potential environmental concerns related to precursor gases and byproducts.

Future advancements in CVD technology promise enhanced material properties, scalability, and integration into emerging applications like flexible electronics and advanced energy solutions, driving innovation.
